CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper is exceptionally clear and well organized: contributions and main claims are presented up front, the method and continuous-relaxation trick are described clearly, and experiments and limitations are laid out logically. Notation and methodological details (including hyperparameters) are reported consistently, and figures/tables (rate–distortion curves, PSNR/MS-SSIM comparisons) effectively communicate results against JPEG and JPEG 2000. The writing is excellent and free of ambiguity, with thorough related-work coverage and explicit statements of limitations, making the paper a pleasure to read. | B(5): The paper is exceptionally clear and well organized: main claims and methodology are presented up front and flow logically into experiments and conclusions. Notation and methodological description are concise and well defined (cascaded transforms, GDN/IGDN, quantization relaxation) and hyperparameters are reported. Figures/tables and evaluation (datasets, baselines, PSNR/MS-SSIM/rate curves) are effectively used to communicate results, and the writing quality is described as excellent with thorough related work coverage.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is exceptionally clear and well organized: contributions and main claims are presented up front, the method and continuous-relaxation trick are described clearly, and experiments and limitations are laid out logically. Notation and methodological details (including hyperparameters) are reported consistently, and figures/tables (rate–distortion curves, PSNR/MS-SSIM comparisons) effectively communicate results against JPEG and JPEG 2000. The writing is excellent and free of ambiguity, with thorough related-work coverage and explicit statements of limitations, making the paper a pleasure to read.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is exceptionally clear and well organized: main claims and methodology are presented up front and flow logically into experiments and conclusions. Notation and methodological description are concise and well defined (cascaded transforms, GDN/IGDN, quantization relaxation) and hyperparameters are reported. Figures/tables and evaluation (datasets, baselines, PSNR/MS-SSIM/rate curves) are effectively used to communicate results, and the writing quality is described as excellent with thorough related work coverage.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable, integrated approach by combining cascaded convolutional transforms with GDN/IGDN nonlinearities and a continuous relaxation of quantization (additive uniform noise) to enable end-to-end SGD optimization for rate–distortion — elements that together form a complete optimized codec and are reported to outperform JPEG/JPEG 2000 in perceptual metrics. The problem (image compression) is well-studied, but the work clearly differentiates its contributions (jointly optimized transforms, the specific GDN/IGDN architecture, and practical entropy coding to report bit rates) and constitutes a non-trivial extension beyond existing methods. Given the reported gains in MS-SSIM and the practical implementation details, the contribution would likely inform experts in the field. | B(4): The paper introduces a notably new end-to-end approach for image compression by jointly optimizing cascaded nonlinear transforms (convolutions + GDN/IGDN) with quantization and entropy modeling, which is a clear methodological departure from classical codecs. The continuous relaxation of quantization (replacing rounding with additive uniform noise) to enable stochastic gradient descent and the practical incorporation of an entropy coder to report actual bit rates are non-trivial technical contributions. The authors explicitly differentiate these elements from prior work and demonstrate gains over JPEG/JPEG 2000, so the techniques go beyond straightforward combinations of known pieces, though they build on established ideas in transform coding and rate–distortion optimization rather than introducing a completely unprecedented problem formulation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable, integrated approach by combining cascaded convolutional transforms with GDN/IGDN nonlinearities and a continuous relaxation of quantization (additive uniform noise) to enable end-to-end SGD optimization for rate–distortion — elements that together form a complete optimized codec and are reported to outperform JPEG/JPEG 2000 in perceptual metrics. The problem (image compression) is well-studied, but the work clearly differentiates its contributions (jointly optimized transforms, the specific GDN/IGDN architecture, and practical entropy coding to report bit rates) and constitutes a non-trivial extension beyond existing methods. Given the reported gains in MS-SSIM and the practical implementation details, the contribution would likely inform experts in the field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a notably new end-to-end approach for image compression by jointly optimizing cascaded nonlinear transforms (convolutions + GDN/IGDN) with quantization and entropy modeling, which is a clear methodological departure from classical codecs. The continuous relaxation of quantization (replacing rounding with additive uniform noise) to enable stochastic gradient descent and the practical incorporation of an entropy coder to report actual bit rates are non-trivial technical contributions. The authors explicitly differentiate these elements from prior work and demonstrate gains over JPEG/JPEG 2000, so the techniques go beyond straightforward combinations of known pieces, though they build on established ideas in transform coding and rate–distortion optimization rather than introducing a completely unprecedented problem formulation.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and are supported by experiments (training on a 6,507‑image ImageNet subset and testing on Kodak and standard images) showing improved PSNR for most cases and substantially better MS‑SSIM versus JPEG/JPEG 2000. The proposed continuous relaxation of quantization (additive uniform noise) is a standard, theoretically plausible way to enable SGD for rate–distortion objectives, and the use of optimized GDN/IGDN transforms with a marginal entropy model is internally consistent. Minor concerns remain: the entropy coding implementation only crudely exploits spatial adaptation and the summary does not report statistical significance tests, and the authors appropriately flag that claims about GDN efficiency are provisional. Overall, the conclusions are supported by the presented evidence, with only minor methodological limitations. | B(4): The summary indicates the main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments (trained transforms on ImageNet subset, tested on Kodak and standard images, reporting PSNR/MS-SSIM and actual bit rates via an entropy coder). The key mathematical device (replacing rounding by additive uniform noise) is a standard, well-motivated continuous relaxation enabling SGD and is reported consistently with implementation details. Experimental methodology appears sound with relevant baselines (JPEG, JPEG 2000) and appropriate metrics, though the authors acknowledge limitations in their entropy coder (crude spatial adaptation) and in perceptual-metric selection, which temper some claims (e.g., PSNR not uniformly improved). Overall the work is technically solid but has minor experimental/implementation limitations that do not undermine the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and are supported by experiments (training on a 6,507‑image ImageNet subset and testing on Kodak and standard images) showing improved PSNR for most cases and substantially better MS‑SSIM versus JPEG/JPEG 2000. The proposed continuous relaxation of quantization (additive uniform noise) is a standard, theoretically plausible way to enable SGD for rate–distortion objectives, and the use of optimized GDN/IGDN transforms with a marginal entropy model is internally consistent. Minor concerns remain: the entropy coding implementation only crudely exploits spatial adaptation and the summary does not report statistical significance tests, and the authors appropriately flag that claims about GDN efficiency are provisional. Overall, the conclusions are supported by the presented evidence, with only minor methodological limitations.

reviewer_b.justification: The summary indicates the main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments (trained transforms on ImageNet subset, tested on Kodak and standard images, reporting PSNR/MS-SSIM and actual bit rates via an entropy coder). The key mathematical device (replacing rounding by additive uniform noise) is a standard, well-motivated continuous relaxation enabling SGD and is reported consistently with implementation details. Experimental methodology appears sound with relevant baselines (JPEG, JPEG 2000) and appropriate metrics, though the authors acknowledge limitations in their entropy coder (crude spatial adaptation) and in perceptual-metric selection, which temper some claims (e.g., PSNR not uniformly improved). Overall the work is technically solid but has minor experimental/implementation limitations that do not undermine the main conclusions.

main_claims: A complete image compression method based on nonlinear transform coding is presented and optimized end-to-end for rate–distortion performance.

Using cascades of linear convolutions and a generalized divisive normalization (GDN) nonlinearity (with an approximate inverse IGDN), jointly optimized transforms plus uniform scalar quantization and an entropy code produce compressed images whose perceptual quality is substantially improved over JPEG and JPEG 2000.

A continuous relaxation of quantization (replacing rounding by additive uniform noise) enables stochastic gradient descent optimization of rate–distortion by approximating discrete entropy and quantization error, and the authors implement an entropy coder to report actual bit rates.

The method yields better rate–distortion performance (by PSNR for most, but not all, test images) and substantially improved perceptual quality (MS-SSIM) across tested images and bit rates compared to JPEG and JPEG 2000.
_summary.methodology: Train cascaded nonlinear analysis and synthesis transforms (convolutions + GDN/IGDN) with a non-parametric model of marginal code distributions, replace quantization by additive uniform noise to obtain a differentiable rate–distortion loss, and jointly optimize transforms and marginal models on ImageNet via stochastic gradient descent; at test time apply uniform scalar quantization and CABAC-based entropy coding to produce actual bit rates.
datasets: Subset of ImageNet (6507 images) used for training
Standard test images: Lena, Barbara, Peppers, Mandrill
limitations: Computational costs for training the models are quite high (stated).
The method yields better PSNR than JPEG/JPEG 2000 for most but not all test images (i.e., not universally superior in PSNR).
They optimized using MSE and set the perceptual transform gp to the identity; they state they do not currently have a reliable perceptual metric for color images.

Their entropy coding implementation only crudely exploits spatial adaptation (due to raster-scan ordering), so gains from adaptive coding are relatively small and likely smaller than JPEG 2000's entropy coding.
Claims about the efficiency advantage of GDN/IGDN over simpler activations are provisional and based on a limited set of experiments (explicitly stated).
_summary.topic_area: Image compression - nonlinear transform coding / deep learning (rate–distortion optimization)
